candidate_problems.text: Which of the four proposed structural properties—dependency density, cross-constraint, error-amplifying feedback, compression of alternatives—are necessary versus merely correlated with hard-to-vary explanations?
Can there exist explanations that are genuinely hard to vary despite being only weakly coupled globally, for example through a single indispensable principle with few explicit dependency loops?
How can 'compression of alternatives' be measured without conflating genuine explanatory constraint with arbitrary formal rigidity or overfitting?
Do feedback-rich systems that kill non-load-bearing combinations quickly also risk eliminating fruitful early variants before their supporting constraint network is developed?
